speaker: interviewee
text: In getting-- you know, misery likes company, I guess, that they feel more comfortable. For the Congress working, there's Anita Achsting, who was eight years old when she's-- was liberated. For six years, she was in hiding. A priest--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she was in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: In hiding. A priest took care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Czechoslovakia, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Poland. She was a baby when they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just give me one second. This is reel number five with Judith Rubinstein. You were telling me about your experience at Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, one morning, while we were under observation-- we didn't work, and Ravensbruck was loaded of people. And there-- still transports came in from Czechoslovakia, they're hidden, whom they given away by good neighbors. And even from Norway, people stayed there. And one day, we were standing outside in a head count early in the morning, and a window opened up, and a little girl looks out-- and there were families-- looks out in the window and said that, oh, you-- I hear you speak Hungarian. I am from Hungary, too. Maybe you know my aunt. And was telling the name. Her name was Vera Grosz, from a big city-- Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as it happens, Vera Grosz was standing in the end of the line with us. And we got all excited. Surely when was-- they say, appel, it's called the head counting-- was over, Vera Grosz knew already that her niece is there. Who else is with you? Some grandma is there. So then we find out-- and surely when it was over, we ran all excited. The hopes were so high that maybe someone else's grandmother is there. We find out from the grandmother that they were one transport from that big city-- two, actually. Two were sent to Austria to work on the fields for Austrian farmers, of course, for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the fall came, and there was nothing to do anymore, they gathered these unfortunate people, they put them on a train, send them to Auschwitz to be killed. By that time, the crematorium wasn't working. It was blown up. And they kept them on the siding for about two days, and they didn't know what to do with them. So they turned back the train and send them to Ravensbruck. So that's how this little girl met the grandmother, and the grandfather arrived to Ravensbruck. Unfortunately, well Vera, our friend, somehow made a change with someone, and joined her mother, and they ended up in Mauthausen. But the grandmother didn't make it, but Vera met her fiancee there. And also, the little girl was picked up by the Red Cross, but this was the Swedish Red Cross, and they took her to Sweden, and she survived. She lives now in Israel. She's alive with her family. And if the crematorium wasn't blown up, certainly she wouldn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was one-- we scored one. We were very happy about it. And Vera Grosz, I met in Italy again, a year later-- two years later. She lives in Israel, too. Tell me about your leaving from Ravensbruck. Tell me about what happened after Ravensbruck, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were taken to Malchow, this was a small town. And there, the outskirts of the town was a big forest, and there were built bunkers, factories. Everything was disguised underground. It was covered up with grass and shrubberies. And I worked there, doing-- in a munition factory, making little bullets. It's very precise, with a very-- had to be very careful. We measured it, and they pressed it, and packed it together. And when it was after midnight, with a hungry stomach, you work all night, it was very difficult to be-- be alert and be careful. By that time, I was with a group of women from all over Europe. It was a little United Nations. The only-- the common language was German that we all understood. But otherwise, we were from all from a different country. And we sung in the middle of the night, not to fall asleep. And telling stories. And that-- really I can say that saved my sanity. And that few hours that we had inside in the block, finally we got home-- one week we worked night shift, and the other worked daytime. And when we were finally on our bed, it was-- it was-- we're trying to keep each other alive and the spirit alive. And they're talking about books, about films, about things what happened in our own country-- and performing, and dancing. There was one young lady from Greece, her name was Eva, she was a ballet dancer. She was performing to us, I mean, in a hungry stomach. But it just was a distraction-- you know, something divide the attention from the-- from the reality what was there, facing. And then came Hanukkah, and we decided that, against all odds, we have to light a Hanukkah candle. And we pulled together that little match, what we got once a week, Sundays, and pulled out the thread from our blanket, and lit the candles-- singing the-- and then telling about what happened thousands of years ago to our ancestors, hoping that they made it-- hopefully that we will find liberation coming our way, too. And it was a close, very close, between the inmates. And that was-- it was a great helping, supporting system. And we worked till end of April. And the bombs were-- I mean, were not falling, but they were flying over. The American bombers, they never hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in Malchow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little bit-- it was no crematorium there. But when-- I mean, when people got sick, they just gathered and send them to Ravensbruck to be killed. But who was healthy, we-- we had no threat, this one. We worked hard, with very little food. But it was more human. We had-- in the barrack, we were 36 woman to a room. And we had a little sink in the-- in the front. And we washed ourselves-- no soap, just with the cold water. We try to keep ourselves clean. And the spirit was very high.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still together with those four women at this point. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From before? No. Unfortunately, I-- I missed them. No, we were separated in Auschwitz. I had another three friends, from my-- close friend of mine, and I was the fourth. And also one more girl from Czechoslovakia made the fifth. And we were together till almost the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three sisters were Lily, Elizabeth, and Sedonia Moskowitz. They were my friends. And the other one was Magda Grosz-- from [NON-ENGLISH]. It was in the Czech part. Well, these three sisters, one is dead, unfortunately. The other two live in New York. But from Magda, I don't know what happened to her. After the war was over, everybody was running, trying to get home and find out what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you all survived together. You  survived the war together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In the camp there was a few deaths, but no violent death as is in Auschwitz. No selection. We-- somehow we made it. And liberation came, it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me before about the bombing in Malchow in April.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was funny, at the moment, and then the alarm was sounded, the SS woman who were our supervisors, they run under shelter, and they let us stay and they work in the factory. And the moment, I mean, there was alarm sounded, and we just plopped on the floor and fell asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they couldn't take it. They were angry as anything. And how can you sleep when is a danger? But we were ready to die. We just didn't think of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that to them-- they were afraid for that. They were cowards, every one of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we saw-- saw that, after the war was over, how they acted cowardly. They all washed themselves-- claimed they didn't do anything. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated in-- on the way-- that's an interesting story. The 1st of May, the Nazis took us out from the camp,

speaker: interviewer
text: 1st of May--

speaker: interviewee
text: We started marching. 1944-- '45, I'm sorry. That was '45 already. The 1st of May. And we were walking about two days. They let us sleep on the ground. It was raining, it was cold, and no food. We were marching. And it's third day-- or rather on the second day, all of a sudden the SS disappeared. That they left us alone, 1,000 woman, left us alone. And we didn't know what to do with ourselves. The bombs were falling, it was-- the tanks were going. The Russian army was behind us. And the Americans came-- when that was in Schwerin, was the place. It's a big city, Schwerin, where they-- the two big armies met. So we were in the middle. And then by that time, we just couldn't walk anymore. Our shoes were falling apart, and we're dead tired. And we arrived to a village. And the edge of the village was a single house. And we decided that we up on-- and-- but no matter what, that's it. So we climbed up on the-- where the animals are, and we dug ourselves into the hay. And we tried to fall asleep. And all of the sudden we heard French speaking-- talking in the yard, and the heavenly smell of coffee coming up. And the hunger was so great that we just couldn't go to sleep. And one by one, we came down. And we find out that they were French prisoners of war-- soldiers. Lovely young men. And they were telling us-- well, they noticed right away because we had a red-- a red cross painted in our back. And the coat, what we were wearing, one sleeve, had to be taken out and change it with someone else's. So I had a navy blue coat, and had one green sleeve in it. That in case, if I want to run away, it was so obvious, that it's so ridiculous, that outfit, I couldn't go far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew who we are, and they were telling us that the Russian army's right behind us. And they probably will get in that night to the town, to the village. And if you know what the Russians are doing to the women? So we didn't know anything, but we were frightened enough to-- when they offered, that if we want to help them to push, they open-- broke into a-- a food depot. And they had bread-- enough bread and coffee. So they gave us a big slice of bread and a mug of coffee. And surely, we very ready to go to the end of the world with them, as long as they-- they giving us food. So we helping them to push that wagon. We went half the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're sleeping in the open field that night, and the following day we're walking more. Around in the early afternoon, we couldn't make it any longer. But that was it. It just-- my leg wouldn't carry me. So-- it was an edge of a forest, it was still no-- no leaves. It was a bare forest. We were sitting down, and nursing our sore feet. All of the sudden, a big sound-- a noise coming from the highway. And we looked out, and we saw the first American flag, and the first tank arrived with American soldiers. And within a minute they-- the forest came alive. People whom we didn't know they were hiding there, came and they were kissing their shoes, the boots of these American soldiers. They get frightened for us. We looked like from another world-- we're ghosts. And they told us that they will send the Red Cross ambulance the following day. It was too early for that. And so we find place in, also another village. And the-- the Germans who lived in the town-- the house, they wouldn't let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of this village that you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The village, I don't know. But that was the outskirt of Schwerin, on the road to Schwerin.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first one that you had told-- this is on the road to Schwerin?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the road to Schwerin. It was about a half a day walk. And they said, OK, you can go up into the haystack. So we were-- we settled down there, hungry-- but, eh, you didn't even feel hunger anymore-- and fall asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early in the morning, Russian prisoner of war soldiers, who were free, came in, and with a bayonet, they're starting to-- you know-- to try at the tape and stuff. That if it's-- who is under? And as we climbed out, and they were surprised to see us. They were looking for Nazi soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we got down. And by that time they-- the German family disappeared. The house was full with goodies, with food and clothes, the best of everything. Probably he must have been a big Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we started to carry a container of meat, conserve, and a loaf of bread. But if it was too heavy to carry, then we just throw it in the ditches. And we're walking and walking, until we got into a place where-- where British soldiers-- because that was where the three big powers came together, just the outskirts of Schwerin. And they allowed us to use one of the-- Nazis left all these big trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made room for ourselves, and we were fed. A lot of people got sick from eating too much the first day. And the following day, we were taken in by the Red Cross to Schwerin. The city was liberated that day-- that the 5th of May. And we stayed in a office that used to be a government office. We stayed there for-- for how many-- six weeks, until I got back to Hungary. So we met these wonderful American soldiers who came-- some, they're Jewish. But all of them, they're wonderful. And they got us into a private villa where there's food and medicine, until we recuperated, we started to feel a little bit better. And then my cousin, whom I find in the camp in Malchow, she was from Czechoslovakia, and the Czech government send a transport-- buses, to pick up their own citizens. So I was not Czech, but my cousin said, you just don't say anything. You come with me. And so I joined them, and we ended up in Prague, where we were registered. And from there, traveled to Bratislava where my cousin find a brother. And one of the girls find a mother, was-- who was in hiding. And from there, I went home the following week. I was very anxious to get home. And I find the only brother who was saved by Wallenberg's papers-- Raoul Wallenberg, the Swedish diplomat, gave out papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got back to Szerencs, you met up with your brother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was there already. My brother was home already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the oldest, who was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he-- he survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, he was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you give us some information about his being saved by Raoul Wallenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he was-- he was lucky. He-- he had a very good captain. He was a good man. And when once they started to hoard the people, they put them into the-- to transport, he told them that you better try to hide, because the Nazis are taking you out. I have no more power. The Nazis are taking you out of Germany. And that was in the winter in '54-- '55, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45-- it's a number, so I'm not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, in December. And then the Russians were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it would've been December of '44, then. It would've been December '44, that this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because liberated when it was end of December, I think Budapest was liberated. I don't know exactly. I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, he-- he somehow, he managed to get out. Unscrewed the wagon, the cattle car, unscrewed with a friend, and pulled out a few planks and got out. And as he was starting to run, the-- the Nazis shoot at him, and they shot his friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother had a Red Cross band in his pocket. He put it on, and took off the yellow star, and he took his friend on his shoulder. And he was telling them, in good German, that his friend is-- was hurt, and he has to take him to the hospital. They let him go. So he dropped him off somewhere, the friend. And he was walking the whole night in the street. Didn't know where to-- where to go. And arrived to the Swiss consulate, they called it Glass House-- it was made out of glass. And they were-- they were well known that they were giving shelter for the-- whoever needed shelter. And he knocked on the door, and they wouldn't let him in. They said it's fullest, it's to the brim. We can't have no more people in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was standing there, waiting, hoping that something will happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning, a truck came, brought bread for these refugees. And opened the gate, and my brother didn't ask anyone-- took a bag of bread on his shoulder, marched in. And when he marched in, he mingled with others. And they never, of course, they never kicked him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stayed. He was liberated there. But he had the papers from Wallenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that helped, because the Nazis, many times, break into their building, and that helped him, that he had a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he actually had a visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a passport, sort of that he is a naturalized Swedish citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So war was over, he came back to-- to-- for our town. Didn't find anyone and anything. He was heartbroken. So he just was hoping, waiting, maybe someone will make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home, it was in the middle of August-- in July, end of July. So, of course, I went straight down. I heard in Budapest already from the Red Cross that he's alive. And I went down. And I find him. But our home was taken by strangers. And there's nothing left of our own home. So we decided that that's it-- I can't stay here. I was running like somebody is behind me. And we went back to Budapest--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we move out of your coming back, tell me a little bit about your reunion between your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very emotional, very emotional. I came in late one night. I came when the trains were not running properly. And-- and the train station, I heard-- I tried to find out where he is staying, nobody heard of him. And then I heard that old friends of mine, two sisters came back, and they knew where they lived. So I knocked, and it was midnight. I knocked in the window, and they let me in through the window. Because someone else lived in the house. And we were talking all night, and crying, and exchanging experiences. And they told me that yes, my brother is in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the following morning I walked-- I-- I went to find him. And it was very emotional, of course. And-- but we couldn't take it. And after a couple of days, and I said, this is not-- no home anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still hoping maybe my father will, maybe, he made it. But-- but the others-- I was positive that they're not alive. There was nothing to really to do there. So we went after off to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just let me stop you one more time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had told me a little bit about your feelings upon coming back to Szerencs, and some of what drove you away and made you realize you couldn't live there. Can you speak to that a little bit more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, there's good neighbors, whom-- they're supposed to be good neighbors. They were all so apologetic. They all-- they didn't do anything. They couldn't help, but sorry, this-- And then what the Russians did to them and, sort of they're telling me stories, console me. And, don't worry, your parents will be back. No, this is just rumors. And I knew that it was not rumors. And when I wanted to tell a little bit of my experiences, by that time, I was fat. I was never in my life was so heavy-- that being on starvation diet for so many months in Germany, the American soldiers started to bring us food, and we were eating like pigs, day and night. And I got a stomach like in nine months pregnant. Wasn't healthy, but this was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came home with a big stomach. They said, look, she-- she was never so fat. How could it be so bad, if you look so good? And then, I just didn't feel like, after a while, to explain my story, because I-- I saw on their faces that they don't believe me. Was just the guilt, or boredom, or whatever, they were not interested. So I decided that this is not my home anymore. This is not my country, they kicked me out. I just don't want to have anything to do with it anymore. So we decided to emigrate. You had told me an anecdote about a woman that you saw walking in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, well, that was-- that was one of really-- the last of-- of-- of the knife cut into me. It was the second day, I walked in the street. It was so strange, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szerencs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szerencs. And a young woman came toward me, wearing my mother's best dress-- a beautiful grey, wool dress, what I made for her. And that dress represented more than just a dress. I got hysterical.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- what shall I do? Pull it down on her, from her? This was-- that was just a dress. But that dress represented my mother, my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to run, and I ran back where we stayed, and I told my brother-- I can't sleep here another night. This is not our home anymore. Let's go. And he reluctantly, because it was really not no-- nowhere to go. So we made it to Budapest and walking the street like a little orphan. I didn't know anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what's the date now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- the date was in-- I came home at end of-- in the middle of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived to Budapest around the middle of August, or you came to Szerencs in the middle of August?

speaker: interviewee
text: First from Budapest, and I stayed over a day-- we were registered by the Red Cross. And they gave me some documents, something, that too should be able to prove that, who am I. And I went down and we stayed about less than a week in my hometown. And then we went back to Budapest. And we went to register by the Zionist organization to go-- to try to get into Palestine. In Budapest I find out that two uncles of mine survived-- one from my father's brother, the other one my mother's brother. And I decided-- we discussed it with my brother that before we leave for good, let's go and see these uncles. Who knows if I will have another chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went down to see my uncle, my father's side, and he just recuperating from typhus-- epidemic was rampant in Hungary. The Russian soldiers brought it in. Russian-- Hungary was occupied by the Russians at that time. And he-- slowly he recuperated. I kept him company, tried to make him feel more comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I was ready to leave him, I find out about my other uncle was also in a very bad state of mind. He was ill health, and very sick emotionally. So I went down there to keep him company for a couple of days. In the meantime, winter set in-- that was October already-- and the aliyahs stopped going. There was nothing going. I couldn't get the proper papers to go to Palestine legally, so we had to wait on illegal transportation. But because of start of the winter, that the boats stopped going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed that winter with my uncle's house. And in the early spring, I-- I met my husband, my future husband, who was married before the war-- had two small children. Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my uncle's house. He lived in the neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mezocsat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're now in--

speaker: interviewee
text: He has-- he has a niece who was with me all the time in the camp, so I knew her well. So that helped also. They decided, that-- these three nieces and three brothers-- decided to-- to leave everything behind, and they're going to Palestine. And my husband had a big flour mill. It was-- it was working day and night, but it had no value. You know-- their lives were missing. And it  was just-- couldn't picture us after settle down there. And the communist regime started to be very pushy and very aggressive, and we had enough of politic. So we decided that-- leave the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he came to offer me that, if I can go with them, with a family of twelve, I was glad to join. And we crossed the border, in Yugoslavia. And from there to Italy. And we stayed in Italy almost three years, because nobody wanted to take us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married, and they took this dangerous little boats, that left from the-- from Genoa, or down in Napoli. And they were very dangerous, and they-- risky, so they took only single people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting and waiting. I was working, as a-- as a dressmaker. I worked for the [NON-ENGLISH]. Where were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Grugliasco-- the outskirts of Turino.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is, what kind-- is that a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Operated by who?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the UNRRA-- United National Relief Fund.

speaker: interviewer
text: Relief and reconstruction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something, yeah. So we were fed, and I was working, and I had made a little bit of supplement, and maybe hoping to get somewhere. In the meantime, in the second year, I had my baby. Robert Eli was born in March, 1948. And the war of independence came in a month later. But right away, the war started in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were scared to take a small baby into the war. The bombs are falling, and God knows what will-- what else will happen. So we decided to wait a little bit longer. And while we were waiting, fur trade commission came from Toronto, and looking for furriers.
